sections.title: The hydrogen atom as a probability cloud

paragraphs.text:
The chapter begins in the simplest possible place, with a single hydrogen atom.
If we want to understand a bond, we must first understand what one electron is doing before any partner arrives.
The wave function is the underlying mathematical description, and its square tells us where the electron is likely to be found.

visuals.label: Figure 1.1
visuals.caption: Three ways of picturing the one s orbital of hydrogen. The point is to see the same electron distribution as contour, cloud, and simple outline.

paragraphs.text:
For hydrogen in its ground state, that electron sits in a one s orbital.
This orbital is spherically symmetric, dense near the nucleus, and already much lower in energy than a separated proton and electron.
Most of the electron population lies very close to the nucleus, which is why hydrogen can look so compact even before we start bonding.

visuals.label: Figure 1.1
visuals.caption: Three ways of picturing the one s orbital of hydrogen. The point is to see the same electron distribution as contour, cloud, and simple outline.

visuals.label: Figure 1.2
visuals.caption: Radial plots for the hydrogen one s orbital. They show both the rapid falloff with distance and the special role of the Bohr radius.

paragraphs.text:
The different pictures in Figures 1 point 1 and 1 point 2 are not rival truths but different ways of seeing the same distribution.
A contour picture emphasizes shape, a cloud picture emphasizes probability, and the radial plots reveal something easy to miss.
Even though the wave function is largest at the nucleus, the radial density reaches its maximum at the Bohr radius, because there is simply more space a little further out.

visuals.label: Figure 1.1
visuals.caption: Three ways of picturing the one s orbital of hydrogen. The point is to see the same electron distribution as contour, cloud, and simple outline.

visuals.label: Figure 1.2
visuals.caption: Radial plots for the hydrogen one s orbital. They show both the rapid falloff with distance and the special role of the Bohr radius.

sections.title: Two hydrogens and the birth of bonding and antibonding orbitals

paragraphs.text:
Once two hydrogen atoms approach one another, we stop thinking in terms of two isolated atomic orbitals and ask for an orbital spread over the whole pair.
The simplest approximation is to write that molecular orbital as a linear combination of the two one s orbitals.
That is the first great move of molecular orbital theory.

visuals.label: Figure 1.3
visuals.caption: The bonding and antibonding molecular orbitals of H two. Matching phase builds density between the nuclei, while opposite phase creates a node.

equations.spoken: Equation 1 point 1 says that the molecular orbital is built as a linear combination of the two atomic orbitals.
equations.note: This is the basic L C A O move. The coefficients say how much each atomic orbital contributes.

paragraphs.text:
When this combination is squared, a new cross term appears.
That term is the whole drama, because it tells us whether the two atomic patterns reinforce each other between the nuclei or cancel there.
If the signs match, electron density builds up in the middle and the two positive nuclei are pulled together.

visuals.label: Figure 1.3
visuals.caption: The bonding and antibonding molecular orbitals of H two. Matching phase builds density between the nuclei, while opposite phase creates a node.

equations.spoken: Equation 1 point 2 shows that squaring the wave function creates a cross term. That extra term is what decides whether overlap is bonding or antibonding.
equations.note: The cross term measures constructive or destructive interference between the two atomic patterns.

paragraphs.text:
If the signs oppose, the middle is depleted and a node appears between the nuclei.
That gives the antibonding sigma star orbital.
In the ground state of H two, both electrons occupy the bonding sigma orbital, so the net effect of bringing the atoms together is stabilization.

visuals.label: Figure 1.3
visuals.caption: The bonding and antibonding molecular orbitals of H two. Matching phase builds density between the nuclei, while opposite phase creates a node.

sections.title: Overlap, alpha and beta, and the energetic shape of a bond

paragraphs.text:
Bonding is only as good as overlap allows it to be.
The overlap integral measures how much the two orbitals occupy the same region of space, and for two one s orbitals it grows steadily as the atoms are brought together.
At infinite separation it is zero, and at full superposition it would be one.

visuals.label: Figure 1.4
visuals.caption: The overlap integral for two hydrogen one s orbitals as the nuclei approach. It turns the intuitive idea of overlap into a measurable quantity.

equations.spoken: Equation 1 point 3 defines the overlap integral.
equations.note: It gives a compact measure of how much the two orbitals share the same region of space.

paragraphs.text:
The textbook symbols alpha and beta give names to two pieces of the energy bookkeeping.
Alpha refers to the energy of an electron in an isolated atomic orbital, while beta captures the extra energetic effect of letting the electron feel both nuclei at once.
Here beta is negative, so it lowers the bonding orbital and raises the antibonding one.

visuals.label: Figure 1.3
visuals.caption: The bonding and antibonding molecular orbitals of H two. Matching phase builds density between the nuclei, while opposite phase creates a node.

equations.spoken: Equation 1 point 4 gives the energy of the bonding molecular orbital.
equations.note: The combination of alpha, beta, and overlap shows how a bonding orbital falls below the atomic reference level.

equations.spoken: Equation 1 point 5 gives the energy of the antibonding molecular orbital.
equations.note: Changing the sign of beta and the overlap term turns stabilization into destabilization.

equations.spoken: Equation 1 point 6 defines the Coulomb integral alpha.
equations.note: Alpha is the energy reference associated with an electron in an isolated atomic orbital.

equations.spoken: Equation 1 point 7 defines the resonance integral beta.
equations.note: Beta captures the energetic effect of allowing the electron to feel both centers at once.

paragraphs.text:
Figure 1 point 5 makes the full balance visible.
Electronic attraction pulls the atoms together, nuclear repulsion pushes them apart, and the familiar well of a bond appears only where those two tendencies find a minimum.
If the antibonding orbital were occupied instead, the atoms would not settle into a bond at all but would be driven apart.

visuals.label: Figure 1.5
visuals.caption: Electronic attraction, nuclear repulsion, and the resulting energy curve for H two. The figure shows why a bond length exists and why antibonding occupation destroys it.

paragraphs.text:
This is also why two helium atoms do not make an ordinary He two molecule.
Once both bonding and antibonding orbitals are filled, the energy penalty of the antibonding occupation slightly outweighs the gain from the bonding one.
Electron correlation softens the picture a little, but it does not rescue the bond.

visuals.label: Figure 1.5
visuals.caption: Electronic attraction, nuclear repulsion, and the resulting energy curve for H two. The figure shows why a bond length exists and why antibonding occupation destroys it.

sections.title: What the hydrogen molecule really looks like

paragraphs.text:
The simple sketches of sigma and sigma star are useful, but the contour plots reveal more.
In the bonding orbital, electron density is drawn in toward the line between the nuclei and then spreads out in an elliptical envelope around them.
In the antibonding orbital, the density is pushed out to the far sides, which is why occupying that orbital helps drive the nuclei apart.

visuals.label: Figure 1.6
visuals.caption: Contour slices through the bonding and antibonding orbitals of H two. The useful contrast is where the electron density is pulled in and where it is pushed out.

visuals.label: Figure 1.7
visuals.caption: Plots of the squared wave functions for the H two orbitals. These make the buildup or depletion of electron population between the nuclei unmistakable.

paragraphs.text:
Plotting the square of the wave function along the bond axis removes the distraction of sign and lets us compare actual electron population.
Now the bonding orbital clearly shows enhanced density in the middle, while the antibonding orbital shows the opposite.
The drawings stop being merely symbolic and start to feel mechanically persuasive.

visuals.label: Figure 1.7
visuals.caption: Plots of the squared wave functions for the H two orbitals. These make the buildup or depletion of electron population between the nuclei unmistakable.

paragraphs.text:
The coefficients in the hydrogen orbitals are equal in magnitude, so each atomic orbital contributes by the familiar factor of zero point seven zero seven.
That symmetry is exact only in the simplest approximation.
Once overlap is handled more carefully, the antibonding coefficients are slightly larger in magnitude, which is another way of saying that antibonding is a little more severe than bonding is generous.

sections.title: Three hydrogens, four hydrogens, and the role of symmetry

paragraphs.text:
With three hydrogen atoms, symmetry steps onto the stage explicitly.
The triangular H three system is built by matching orbitals that have the same symmetry with respect to the relevant mirror plane.
A symmetric one s type combination can mix with the symmetric sigma orbital, while the antisymmetric sigma star has to stand apart unless another antisymmetric partner is available.

visuals.label: Figure 1.8
visuals.caption: Interacting orbitals for triangular H three. The key lesson is that only orbitals of matching symmetry can mix.

visuals.label: Figure 1.9
visuals.caption: Relative orbital energies for triangular and linear H three. Geometry changes overlap and therefore redistributes stabilization and antibonding.

paragraphs.text:
The result is a set of three molecular orbitals with different nodal patterns and different energetic roles.
H three plus is stabilized because its two electrons both fall into the lowest of them.
The neutral radical still comes out overall bonding, but H three minus loses that balance because the antibonding occupancy becomes too costly.

visuals.label: Figure 1.8
visuals.caption: Interacting orbitals for triangular H three. The key lesson is that only orbitals of matching symmetry can mix.

visuals.label: Figure 1.9
visuals.caption: Relative orbital energies for triangular and linear H three. Geometry changes overlap and therefore redistributes stabilization and antibonding.

paragraphs.text:
H four teaches the same lesson more sternly.
When two filled H two units are brought together, one bonding combination is gained and one antibonding combination is gained with it.
Since filled orbitals generally repel one another more than they stabilize, two H two molecules do not fuse into a stable H four molecule, and that simple fact foreshadows a major principle of chemical reactivity.

visuals.label: Figure 1.10
visuals.caption: The orbitals of tetrahedral H four. It illustrates why combining two filled H two units gives repulsion rather than a new stable molecule.

sections.title: Carbon enters with two s and two p

paragraphs.text:
Carbon immediately enriches the story because its valence shell contains both s and p orbitals.
The one s core is too tight and too deep to matter for ordinary bonding, so attention shifts to the two s and the three two p orbitals.
These are the orbitals out of which organic structure will be built.

The two s orbital still has spherical symmetry, but it now contains a radial node.
That node creates an inner and an outer region, though for bonding purposes the outer region does almost all the visible work.
This is why the two s orbital is often drawn simply as a circle even though its real wave function is more subtle.

visuals.label: Figure 1.11
visuals.caption: The carbon two s orbital in wave function, contour, and conventional form. It is a reminder that the simple circle hides a radial node.

paragraphs.text:
The three two p orbitals point along the three axes and sit higher in energy than the two s because their electrons are, on average, further from the nucleus.
Their shapes are directional, their signs matter, and their angular character makes them the great architects of sigma and pi bonding.
Once carbon appears, geometry is no longer an ornament but part of the electronic logic itself.

visuals.label: Figure 1.12
visuals.caption: A carbon two p x orbital in several representations. The figure makes the directional character and sign pattern of p orbitals visible.

sections.title: How carbon overlaps with its neighbors

paragraphs.text:
A p orbital can overlap with an s orbital head on to make a sigma interaction, or with another p orbital head on to make a stronger kind of sigma interaction between aligned lobes.
But those overlaps are not monotonic.
They rise, reach a maximum, and then fall again as the front lobe begins to interfere with the back lobe.

visuals.label: Figure 1.13
visuals.caption: Overlap integrals for sigma overlap involving carbon p orbitals. It shows why distance and alignment both control bond strength.

paragraphs.text:
This is a quietly important point.
Bringing atoms closer does not guarantee better bonding once the phases begin to cancel in the wrong places.
The same orbital can help at one distance and frustrate at another.

visuals.label: Figure 1.13
visuals.caption: Overlap integrals for sigma overlap involving carbon p orbitals. It shows why distance and alignment both control bond strength.

paragraphs.text:
Angle matters too.
For a one s orbital on hydrogen approaching a two p orbital on carbon, the overlap is proportional to the cosine of the angle of approach.
Head on gives the strongest sigma bonding, while the nodal plane of the p orbital gives none at all.

visuals.label: Figure 1.13
visuals.caption: Overlap integrals for sigma overlap involving carbon p orbitals. It shows why distance and alignment both control bond strength.

sections.title: Methane without hybrids

paragraphs.text:
The book first builds methane without invoking hybridization.
A tetrahedral H four unit is classified by symmetry, and its orbitals are matched with the two s and two p orbitals of carbon.
Only orbitals of the same symmetry are allowed to mix, and that already gives four bonding molecular orbitals and four antibonding counterparts.

visuals.label: Figure 1.14
visuals.caption: Methane built from the orbitals of tetrahedral H four and a carbon atom. The figure emphasizes symmetry matching rather than local bond pictures.

paragraphs.text:
One of the filled orbitals lies lower because it draws on the lower energy two s orbital of carbon.
The other three, derived from the two p set, are equal in energy.
So even in methane, which chemists often treat as the very emblem of four identical bonds, the deeper molecular picture is already more layered than that convenient slogan suggests.

visuals.label: Figure 1.14
visuals.caption: Methane built from the orbitals of tetrahedral H four and a carbon atom. The figure emphasizes symmetry matching rather than local bond pictures.

paragraphs.text:
Another subtle point now appears.
No single molecular orbital corresponds to one single C H bond.
Each hydrogen is held to carbon by contributions from more than one filled orbital, and Figure 1 point 15 makes that distributed bonding visible in a more realistic shape than the conventional textbook lobes.

visuals.label: Figure 1.15
visuals.caption: Realistic contour surfaces for the four filled orbitals of methane. It shows how distributed the bonding is compared with simple structural lines.

sections.title: Methylene and why bent can beat linear

paragraphs.text:
Methylene, C H two, is simpler in atom count than methane and richer in electronic drama.
Now the symmetry analysis produces one set of orbitals from the symmetric H H combination and another from the antisymmetric one.
The carbon two p y orbital is left unmatched and therefore nonbonding.

visuals.label: Figure 1.16
visuals.caption: Methylene built from H two and carbon orbitals. The figure highlights the bent bonding pattern and the unmatched p orbital.

visuals.label: Figure 1.17
visuals.caption: Interactions of carbon two s and two p z with the H H orbital of the same symmetry. It clarifies why three orbitals emerge from three ingredients.

equations.spoken: Equation 1 point 8 writes a molecular orbital as a three term combination.
equations.note: This is the algebraic form needed when three orbitals of the same symmetry are mixing, as in methylene.

paragraphs.text:
Because the two s and two p z orbitals of carbon both share the right symmetry with the bonding H H combination, three orbitals must be built from three ingredients.
That is why the picture is more entangled than methane.
The lowest orbital remains largely s like, the next one has strong p character and bulges in the molecular plane, and the highest is antibonding overall.

visuals.label: Figure 1.17
visuals.caption: Interactions of carbon two s and two p z with the H H orbital of the same symmetry. It clarifies why three orbitals emerge from three ingredients.

paragraphs.text:
Most importantly, the bent structure wins over the linear one.
In the linear geometry the relevant p orbital would place the hydrogens in its node and would contribute nothing to bonding.
By bending, the molecule allows that orbital to help, and the energy drops.

visuals.label: Figure 1.16
visuals.caption: Methylene built from H two and carbon orbitals. The figure highlights the bent bonding pattern and the unmatched p orbital.

visuals.label: Figure 1.17
visuals.caption: Interactions of carbon two s and two p z with the H H orbital of the same symmetry. It clarifies why three orbitals emerge from three ingredients.

sections.title: Hybridization as a useful but chosen picture

paragraphs.text:
Chemists often prefer a more localized story, and this is where hybridization enters.
Instead of first building the molecular orbitals from separate s and p orbitals, we first mix the carbon orbitals among themselves into new directional hybrids.
That is a change in representation, not a change in the molecule.

visuals.label: Figure 1.18
visuals.caption: The standard sp, sp two, and sp three hybrid sets. The point is not just the algebraic mixing, but the directional geometry each set supports.

paragraphs.text:
The familiar cases now line up cleanly.
Half s plus half p gives two s p hybrids, one third s with suitable p contributions gives three s p two hybrids, and one quarter s with three quarters total p character gives four s p three hybrids.
Each set is tuned to a geometry, digonal, trigonal, or tetrahedral.

visuals.label: Figure 1.18
visuals.caption: The standard sp, sp two, and sp three hybrid sets. The point is not just the algebraic mixing, but the directional geometry each set supports.

paragraphs.text:
Figure 1 point 19 is a salutary corrective to the cartoon version.
A real s p three hybrid is fatter than the usual drawing, its back lobe wraps behind the nucleus, and the nucleus sits inside the back lobe rather than neatly in front of it.
The conventional hybrid is helpful, but only if we keep that mental reservation alive.

visuals.label: Figure 1.19
visuals.caption: A contour section through an sp three hybrid on carbon. It corrects the thin cartoon by showing the fat front lobe and the hidden back lobe.

visuals.label: Figure 1.20
visuals.caption: Bonding and antibonding orbitals of a C H bond built from a hydrogen one s orbital and a carbon sp three hybrid.

visuals.label: Figure 1.21
visuals.caption: Methane drawn from sp three hybridized orbitals and as conventional bonds. It shows what the hybrid picture gains in local clarity and loses in orbital detail.

paragraphs.text:
That reservation matters philosophically as well as technically.
It is better to say that a carbon center is tetrahedral or trigonal than to speak as though the atom itself were literally rehybridizing inside nature.
Hybridization is our economical picture, and it is best used when it clarifies without deceiving.

visuals.label: Figure 1.20
visuals.caption: Bonding and antibonding orbitals of a C H bond built from a hydrogen one s orbital and a carbon sp three hybrid.

visuals.label: Figure 1.21
visuals.caption: Methane drawn from sp three hybridized orbitals and as conventional bonds. It shows what the hybrid picture gains in local clarity and loses in orbital detail.

sections.title: Ethane and the hidden complexity of a sigma bond

paragraphs.text:
Ethane introduces the C C bond, and with it a fuller orbital crowd.
The occupied set contains seven bonding molecular orbitals, even before we worry about the unfilled antibonding ones.
So the force holding the two carbons together is not concentrated in a single line on the page, even though the structural formula tempts us to think so.

visuals.label: Figure 1.22
visuals.caption: The bonding orbitals of ethane and selected antibonding ones. It shows that a single C C bond is made from several molecular contributions.

paragraphs.text:
The major part of the C C sigma bond comes from an orbital of x type, strongly focused along the carbon carbon axis.
There is also a contribution from the largely s based pair usually called sigma s and sigma s prime.
Meanwhile the two p y and two p z sets overlap sideways and generate weak pi like bonding and antibonding contributions that mostly cancel with respect to the C C bond while still helping the C H framework.

visuals.label: Figure 1.22
visuals.caption: The bonding orbitals of ethane and selected antibonding ones. It shows that a single C C bond is made from several molecular contributions.

visuals.label: Figure 1.23
visuals.caption: Comparison of sigma and pi overlap integrals for carbon p orbitals. The main lesson is that pi overlap is weaker at normal bond lengths.

visuals.label: Figure 1.24
visuals.caption: A major orbital contribution to the C C sigma bond of ethane. It is the localized sigma and sigma star picture extracted from the fuller set.

paragraphs.text:
That sideways contribution is worth noticing because it prepares the mind for later comparisons.
Pi overlap is real even in ethane, but it is weak because sideways overlap cannot hold the nuclei together as directly as head on sigma overlap can.
The localized sigma bond picture is therefore acceptable as a simplification, but the fuller orbital set explains why it is only a simplification.

visuals.label: Figure 1.23
visuals.caption: Comparison of sigma and pi overlap integrals for carbon p orbitals. The main lesson is that pi overlap is weaker at normal bond lengths.

visuals.label: Figure 1.24
visuals.caption: A major orbital contribution to the C C sigma bond of ethane. It is the localized sigma and sigma star picture extracted from the fuller set.

sections.title: Ethylene and the essence of Hückel theory

paragraphs.text:
Ethylene is the place where the sigma framework and the pi system separate cleanly enough to become a method.
Five low lying bonding orbitals make up the sigma framework, while the remaining pair of p z orbitals form the pi bond and its antibonding partner.
That separation is the essence of Hückel theory.

visuals.label: Figure 1.25
visuals.caption: The bonding orbitals of ethylene and one antibonding orbital. It separates the sigma framework from the pi bond.

visuals.label: Figure 1.26
visuals.caption: A localized picture of the C equals C pi bond and its antibonding partner.

paragraphs.text:
The pi bond is localized on the two carbons because the hydrogens sit in the nodal plane of the p z orbitals and therefore do not mix into this part of the system.
The coefficients return to the familiar hydrogen like values of zero point seven zero seven and zero point seven zero seven with opposite sign in pi star.
A pi bond is therefore, in one sense, the cleanest small molecular orbital problem after H two.

visuals.label: Figure 1.26
visuals.caption: A localized picture of the C equals C pi bond and its antibonding partner.

paragraphs.text:
The electron in a box picture is not introduced here as ornament but as a compressed way of remembering nodal order and coefficients.
With only two p orbitals, the one hundred eighty degree sine curve gives the bonding pi orbital and the three hundred sixty degree curve gives pi star.
Figures 1 point 27 through 1 point 29 make the same point in three increasingly concrete ways, from sign pattern to contour map to wire mesh.

visuals.label: Figure 1.27
visuals.caption: The pi orbitals of ethylene and the electron in a box analogy. It turns nodal pattern into a memorable sine wave picture.

visuals.label: Figure 1.28
visuals.caption: Contour sections through the pi and pi star orbitals of ethylene. The figure makes sideways overlap look physically real.

visuals.label: Figure 1.29
visuals.caption: Wire mesh surfaces for the ethylene pi and pi star orbitals. They give the three dimensional shape that the simpler sketches suppress.

sections.title: The allyl system and the first genuinely delocalized frontier orbital

paragraphs.text:
With allyl, the theory becomes recognizably organic.
The allyl cation, radical, and anion share the same sigma framework and differ only in the number of electrons placed into a three orbital pi system.
This is why one set of molecular orbitals can illuminate three different reactive intermediates at once.

visuals.caption: The allyl cation drawn in localized form. It is useful as a conventional entry point into a delocalized system.

visuals.label: 1.4c
visuals.caption: A delocalized drawing of the allyl cation. It helps prevent the common mistake of treating resonance forms as a reaction sequence.

paragraphs.text:
The resonance drawings are both useful and dangerous.
They are useful because they highlight where charge or odd electron density is concentrated, but they are dangerous if one starts to imagine them as a reaction that shuttles back and forth.
Diagram 1 point 4 c is the corrective reminder that the p system is delocalized even when we temporarily draw it in localized forms.

visuals.label: 1.4c
visuals.caption: A delocalized drawing of the allyl cation. It helps prevent the common mistake of treating resonance forms as a reaction sequence.

paragraphs.text:
Mathematically, the allyl pi orbital is a three term combination, and physically that produces three levels.
The lowest is bonding across the whole chain, the middle one is nonbonding, and the highest is antibonding.
The middle orbital is especially elegant, because its node falls on the central carbon atom itself, forcing the coefficient there to zero.

visuals.label: Figure 1.30
visuals.caption: The three pi orbitals of the allyl system. The central nonbonding orbital is the especially important feature.

equations.spoken: Equation 1 point 9 is the three term allyl combination.
equations.note: The same formal structure now generates the three pi orbitals of the allyl system.

paragraphs.text:
The circle construction then gives the energies almost at a glance.
It explains why the middle allyl orbital sits at the alpha level and why the lower and upper orbitals lie symmetrically below and above it.
Equation 1 point 10 packages that geometry into a compact formula.

visuals.label: Figure 1.31
visuals.caption: A circle construction for the energies of the pi orbitals in ethylene and allyl. It turns orbital energies into simple geometry.

equations.spoken: Equation 1 point 10 converts the circle construction for a linear conjugated system into a direct energy formula.
equations.note: It gives the Hückel energy levels for the pi orbitals of a chain of n atoms.

paragraphs.text:
An alternative route reaches the same result by letting an extra p orbital interact with the pi and pi star orbitals of ethylene.
That construction is worth dwelling on because it shows how a new nonbonding orbital can emerge as a compromise between one bonding interaction and one antibonding interaction.
The allyl system is the first clear case in which delocalization is not just pretty but diagnostically powerful.

visuals.label: Figure 1.32
visuals.caption: An extra p orbital interacting separately with pi and pi star of ethylene. It is a stepping stone toward the allyl pattern.

visuals.label: Figure 1.33
visuals.caption: The allyl system rebuilt by letting a p orbital interact with pi and pi star. It shows how the nonbonding orbital emerges.

paragraphs.text:
Once the coefficients are squared, the charge distribution follows.
In the cation, electron deficiency is concentrated at the two ends, and in the anion the extra electron density is likewise concentrated at the two ends.
That is why the localized drawings work as frontier orbital mnemonics even while the real orbital picture is delocalized.

visuals.label: Figure 1.34
visuals.caption: Wave functions and one electron populations for the allyl orbitals. Squaring the coefficients turns orbital shape into charge distribution.

visuals.label: Figure 1.35
visuals.caption: Total pi electron populations in the allyl cation and anion. The end concentrated charge pattern becomes immediately visible.

visuals.label: Figure 1.36
visuals.caption: Wire mesh forms of the allyl pi orbitals. The figure shows that the nonbonding orbital is only approximately nonbonding.

sections.title: Butadiene and the first real gain from conjugation

paragraphs.text:
Butadiene extends the same logic from three p orbitals to four.
Now there are four levels, with zero, one, two, and three nodes respectively.
The lowest two are filled in the ground state, and they are not equivalent in what they do to electron density.

visuals.label: Figure 1.37
visuals.caption: The four pi molecular orbitals of butadiene with coefficients and nodes. It is the basic map for conjugation across four carbons.

equations.spoken: Equation 1 point 11 is the four term linear combination used for butadiene.
equations.note: Four p orbitals produce four molecular orbitals with four coefficient sets.

paragraphs.text:
The first orbital, psi one, is bonding everywhere and therefore pulls electron density strongly into the middle of the chain.
The next one, psi two, still counts as overall bonding, but the node between carbon two and carbon three pushes density away from the middle and toward the ends.
So even before we talk about reactivity, the nodal pattern is already shaping where electrons prefer to live.

visuals.label: Figure 1.37
visuals.caption: The four pi molecular orbitals of butadiene with coefficients and nodes. It is the basic map for conjugation across four carbons.

paragraphs.text:
Equation 1 point 12 gives the coefficients directly from the sine pattern.
It is more than a calculation trick.
It tells us that the coefficients are not arbitrary decorations on the orbitals but a quantitative measure of how strongly each atom participates in each molecular pattern.

visuals.label: Figure 1.37
visuals.caption: The four pi molecular orbitals of butadiene with coefficients and nodes. It is the basic map for conjugation across four carbons.

equations.spoken: Equation 1 point 12 gives the coefficient on atom j in molecular orbital r of a linear conjugated system.
equations.note: It turns the electron in a box picture into actual numbers for Hückel coefficients.

paragraphs.text:
There is also a more intuitive construction in which two ethylene pi systems are allowed to interact.
From that perspective, conjugation lowers the lowest orbital more than it raises the second one, and that asymmetry is the energetic secret of stabilization.
Figures 1 point 38 through 1 point 40 make this especially clear.

visuals.label: Figure 1.38
visuals.caption: Primary interactions of the pi orbitals of two ethylenes. It is a useful intermediate picture for understanding butadiene.

visuals.label: Figure 1.39
visuals.caption: Energies of the pi orbitals of ethylene and butadiene by orbital interaction. The key point is that the lowest orbital drops more than the next one rises.

visuals.label: Figure 1.40
visuals.caption: A geometric comparison of pi orbital energies in ethylene and butadiene. It translates conjugative stabilization into simple numerical steps.

paragraphs.text:
The gain is not infinite, but it is real.
Butadiene is lower in pi energy than two isolated double bonds would be, and the wire mesh orbitals make that gain look physically plausible rather than merely algebraic.
By now the HOMO and the LUMO are no longer rhetorical labels but visible actors with distinct shapes and coefficients.

visuals.label: Figure 1.41
visuals.caption: Wire mesh views of the butadiene pi orbitals in the s trans conformation. They show where the HOMO and LUMO really place their lobes.

sections.title: Longer polyenes and the shrinking HOMO LUMO gap

paragraphs.text:
Once the pattern is clear, longer linear conjugated systems are conceptually easy even if the bookkeeping grows.
The lower levels keep descending, the highest occupied level rises, and the lowest unoccupied one falls.
The HOMO and LUMO therefore move toward each other as the chain length increases.

visuals.label: Figure 1.42
visuals.caption: Energies and coefficients for the smaller linear conjugated systems. The trend in HOMO and LUMO positions is the main thing to notice.

paragraphs.text:
That narrowing gap has a direct optical consequence.
Less energy is needed to promote an electron from the HOMO to the LUMO, so longer polyenes absorb at longer wavelengths.
The mathematics becomes visibly chemical.

visuals.label: Figure 1.42
visuals.caption: Energies and coefficients for the smaller linear conjugated systems. The trend in HOMO and LUMO positions is the main thing to notice.

paragraphs.text:
If simple Hückel theory were pushed to an infinite polyene without correction, the gap would collapse to zero and the chain would become metallic.
Real molecules refuse that limit.
They undergo a Peierls or Jahn Teller type distortion, alternating their bond lengths and reopening a gap.

This is one of the chapter's recurring lessons.
A useful model may be profoundly right about trends and still need correction at an extreme.
In long polyenes and conducting polymers, that correction becomes the chemistry.

sections.title: Aromaticity and antiaromaticity

paragraphs.text:
Benzene does not merely continue the polyene story.
It bends it into a ring and creates a new electronic regime.
With six p orbitals arranged cyclically, one gets a lowest orbital with no node, two degenerate orbitals with one node each, two degenerate antibonding orbitals with two nodes each, and one highest orbital with three nodes.

visuals.label: Figure 1.43
visuals.caption: The six pi orbitals of benzene in side and top view. It shows the node pattern behind aromatic stabilization.

visuals.label: Figure 1.44
visuals.caption: A circle construction for the energies of the benzene pi orbitals. Degenerate pairs appear naturally from the ring geometry.

paragraphs.text:
The total pi bonding of benzene comes out greater than that of three isolated double bonds and even greater than that of open chain hexatriene.
That extra lowering is the orbital basis of aromatic stabilization.
The circle construction and the comparison with closing hexatriene into a ring both point to the same conclusion.

visuals.label: Figure 1.44
visuals.caption: A circle construction for the energies of the benzene pi orbitals. Degenerate pairs appear naturally from the ring geometry.

visuals.label: Figure 1.45
visuals.caption: The drop in pi energy when hexatriene is closed into benzene. It is an interaction view of aromatic stabilization.

paragraphs.text:
The ring current belongs to the same story.
Because the delocalized electrons can circulate, aromatic rings generate a diamagnetic anisotropy that moves outer protons downfield and inner protons of large annulenes upfield.
Magnetic behavior here is not an afterthought but a fingerprint of delocalized electronic motion.

Four n systems behave very differently.
Square cyclobutadiene has one strongly bonding orbital and then a pair of nonbonding orbitals rather than a second deep gain in stabilization.
Relative to butadiene, the cyclic four electron system loses the energetic advantage of ordinary conjugation.

visuals.label: Figure 1.46
visuals.caption: The pi orbitals of square cyclobutadiene. The pair of nonbonding orbitals is the crucial antiaromatic feature.

visuals.label: Figure 1.47
visuals.caption: No net gain in pi energy on going from butadiene to cyclobutadiene. The figure contrasts antiaromatic closure with aromatic closure.

paragraphs.text:
That is why antiaromatic systems are so often both high in energy and unusually reactive.
Their HOMO is high, their LUMO is low, and the gap between them is uncomfortably small.
Cyclobutadiene then escapes by a Jahn Teller distortion into a rectangle with alternating bond lengths, lowering one of the formerly degenerate levels and raising the other.

visuals.label: Figure 1.48
visuals.caption: The three lowest pi orbitals of rectangular cyclobutadiene. It illustrates the Jahn Teller distortion that splits the former degeneracy.

sections.title: Cyclopentadienyl, cyclopentadienyl cation, and the frontier gap

paragraphs.text:
The cyclopentadienyl anion and cation offer a compact contrast between aromatic and antiaromatic behavior.
In the anion, six pi electrons fill three lowered orbitals and produce an aromatic stabilization very similar in spirit to benzene.
In the cation, only four electrons are available and the cyclic pattern fails to deliver that bonus.

visuals.label: Figure 1.49
visuals.caption: The energies and coefficients of the cyclopentadienyl pi system. It is the compact comparison between aromatic anion and antiaromatic cation.

paragraphs.text:
This contrast is not just thermodynamic.
Aromatic systems tend to have a substantial HOMO LUMO gap, while antiaromatic systems have a vanishing or at least a troublingly small one.
That difference helps explain why aromatic rings feel electronically self contained and antiaromatic rings feel eager for reaction or distortion.

visuals.label: Figure 1.49
visuals.caption: The energies and coefficients of the cyclopentadienyl pi system. It is the compact comparison between aromatic anion and antiaromatic cation.

paragraphs.text:
The ring current follows the same division.
Aromatic rings support the familiar diamagnetic circulation, while four n systems tend toward a paramagnetic response because low energy orbital promotion between nearly degenerate frontier levels becomes possible.
The magnetic and energetic criteria converge on the same judgment.

sections.title: Homoaromaticity and spiro conjugation

paragraphs.text:
Conjugation does not always require direct bond to bond continuity.
If p orbitals can still overlap through space across an interruption, a system may be homoaromatic.
The interrupted framework then behaves as though a hidden bridge of overlap had survived.

visuals.label: Figure 1.50
visuals.caption: Relative energies of local, homoaromatic, and homoantiaromatic arrangements. The figure distinguishes stabilization from mere transition structure effects.

paragraphs.text:
The energy diagrams in Figure 1 point 50 distinguish three possibilities.
Some interrupted systems are genuinely stabilized by through space cyclic overlap.
Some merely pass through a potentially homoaromatic geometry on the way between localized structures.
Others are penalized because the interrupted cyclic overlap would be antiaromatic.
This is one of those places where orbital logic explains not just a structure but a barrier.

visuals.label: Figure 1.50
visuals.caption: Relative energies of local, homoaromatic, and homoantiaromatic arrangements. The figure distinguishes stabilization from mere transition structure effects.

paragraphs.text:
Spiro conjugation is weaker still, because the two p systems lie at right angles and overlap only through selected lobes with matching symmetry.
Yet even this small overlap can measurably lower or raise the energy depending on whether the total electron count falls on the aromatic or antiaromatic side.
The effect is faint, but it is real enough to be chemically legible.

visuals.label: Figure 1.51
visuals.caption: Pi molecular orbitals of an aromatic spiroheptatriene. It shows how weak orthogonal overlap can still lower the energy.

visuals.label: Figure 1.52
visuals.caption: Pi molecular orbitals of an antiaromatic spirononatetraene. Here the same kind of overlap raises the overall energy instead.

sections.title: Strained sigma bonds in cyclopropane and cyclobutane

paragraphs.text:
Cyclopropane reveals that a sigma bond can be bent so far from ideal head on overlap that it begins to imitate pi chemistry.
In the simple hybrid picture, the carbon orbitals can only meet imperfectly, so the C C bonds fall somewhere between ordinary sigma and sideways pi overlap.
That is why cyclopropanes are neither as inert as alkanes nor as free as alkenes, but occupy a curious middle ground.

The Walsh description makes this kinship even sharper.
It displays a high lying occupied orbital with substantial p character and the right symmetry to overlap with neighboring unsaturated systems.
That is why a cyclopropyl group can participate in conjugation more effectively than a casual structural formula would suggest.

visuals.label: Figure 1.53
visuals.caption: A simplified set of occupied Walsh orbitals for cyclopropane. The figure makes the p like character of the strained C C bonds explicit.

paragraphs.text:
Even the magnetic behavior is telling.
Cyclopropane shows an aromatic like ring current pattern in the C H bond system, but the geometry places its protons in a region of reduced field, so they appear unusually far upfield in proton N M R.
Cyclobutane shows a related but weaker and partly opposite effect because its four bond pattern resembles an antiaromatic arrangement and the ring can buckle to relieve the strain.

sections.title: When the two atoms do not start at the same energy

paragraphs.text:
So far the cleanest interaction diagrams have paired orbitals of comparable energy.
Heteronuclear bonds force us to relax that comfort.
Electronegativity and calculated orbital energies now become crucial because the two partners do not meet on equal terms.

visuals.label: Figure 1.54
visuals.caption: Allen electronegativity values and related carbon hybrid values. It provides a quick scale for orbital inequality across elements.

visuals.label: Figure 1.55
visuals.caption: Electronegativity drawn as a third dimension of the periodic table. It makes the ordering vivid at a glance.

visuals.label: Figure 1.56
visuals.caption: Calculated valence orbital energies for first and second row elements. This is the more direct orbital version of electronegativity.

visuals.label: Figure 1.57
visuals.caption: Orbital energies for hybrid orbitals. It shows how adding s character lowers an orbital.

paragraphs.text:
In a C Cl sigma bond, the chlorine orbital starts lower in energy, so the bonding molecular orbital is polarized toward chlorine and the antibonding orbital is polarized toward carbon.
The covalent stabilization is therefore less than in a symmetric C C bond, but the bond as a whole is strengthened by electrostatic attraction between partial charges.
This is why a C Cl bond can be fairly strong homolytically and yet much easier to break heterolytically than a C C bond.

visuals.label: Figure 1.58
visuals.caption: A major part of the C Cl sigma bond. The unequal orbital energies produce polarization toward chlorine.

visuals.label: Figure 1.59
visuals.caption: Filled orbitals and the lowest unfilled orbital of methyl chloride, shown with and without hybridization. The LUMO is polarized toward carbon.

visuals.label: Figure 1.60
visuals.caption: An intentionally oversimplified ionic bond picture for sodium fluoride. It clarifies the meaning of the ionic energy term.

visuals.label: Figure 1.61
visuals.caption: Wire mesh surfaces for the major C Cl bonding orbital and the LUMO of methyl chloride.

equations.spoken: Equation 1 point 13 gives the lowered energy level for the C chlorine bonding combination.
equations.note: The important point is that unequal orbital energies make the stabilization depend on both overlap and the energy gap between the partners.

equations.spoken: Equation 1 point 14 gives the raised energy level for the antibonding C chlorine combination.
equations.note: It is the partner to Equation 1 point 13 and shows the same dependence on overlap and orbital mismatch.

paragraphs.text:
Methyllithium flips the polarity.
Now carbon is the lower energy partner, so the highest occupied bonding orbital is polarized toward carbon and the corresponding low unoccupied orbital toward lithium.
That helps explain why organolithium compounds behave like strongly carbanion like donors even though the bond still retains covalent character.

visuals.label: Figure 1.62
visuals.caption: Major orbital interactions for methyl chloride, methyl fluoride, and methyllithium. It compares how changing the partner changes the dominant mixing.

visuals.label: Figure 1.63
visuals.caption: A contributory part of the Li C sigma bond. The polarization now points toward carbon rather than away from it.

visuals.label: Figure 1.64
visuals.caption: Filled orbitals and one low unfilled orbital of methyllithium. It is the covalent picture behind the carbanion like behavior.

visuals.label: Figure 1.65
visuals.caption: Wire mesh views of the HOMO and LUMO of methyllithium. They show how the frontier orbitals are distributed across carbon and lithium.

paragraphs.text:
A carbonyl pi bond follows the same unequal energy logic in a cleaner Hückel setting.
The bonding pi orbital is polarized toward oxygen, while pi star is polarized toward carbon.
That is the orbital reason nucleophiles attack the carbon of a carbonyl group so readily.

visuals.label: Figure 1.66
visuals.caption: A C equals O pi bond and its antibonding partner. The bond is polarized toward oxygen, while pi star is polarized toward carbon.

visuals.label: Figure 1.67
visuals.caption: Wire mesh plots of the formaldehyde pi and pi star orbitals. These make carbonyl polarization spatially visible.

visuals.label: Figure 1.68
visuals.caption: Electron population contours for the formaldehyde pi and pi star orbitals. The oxygen side is more compact, not merely larger.

equations.spoken: Equation 1 point 15 adjusts the alpha value for a heteroatom X.
equations.note: In simple Hückel treatments, the parameter h shifts the orbital energy relative to carbon.

equations.spoken: Equation 1 point 16 adjusts the beta value for a carbon heteroatom pi bond.
equations.note: The parameter k scales the interaction strength relative to a carbon carbon reference bond.

sections.title: Heteroaromatic systems, the tau bond model, and experimental checks

paragraphs.text:
Pyrrole and pyridine show that aromaticity easily survives the introduction of heteroatoms.
The cyclic lowering remains, but the orbitals are skewed because the heteroatom pulls electron density toward itself in some levels and pushes it away in others.
The familiar aromatic patterns are preserved, but they are no longer perfectly democratic around the ring.

visuals.label: Figure 1.69
visuals.caption: Pi molecular orbitals of pyrrole, pyridine, and the pyridinium ion. The aromatic pattern survives but is skewed by heteroatom electronegativity.

paragraphs.text:
The tau bond model offers an alternative way of describing alkenes and conjugated systems using only bent sigma like bonds between s p three carbon centers.
It is legitimate and sometimes suggestive for stereochemical thinking.
But for ordinary questions of conjugation, nodal structure, and frontier orbitals, the Hückel picture remains clearer and more powerful.

The final pages of the chapter are a reminder that molecular orbital theory is not only a visual language but an experimentally answerable one.
Ultraviolet spectroscopy tracks the HOMO to LUMO gap.
N M R coupling constants reveal how spin information is transmitted through occupied orbitals.
Photoelectron spectroscopy measures the energies of filled orbitals rather directly.
E S R reports unpaired electron distribution in radicals.
The theory survives because the molecule keeps answering in the same accent.

visuals.label: Figure 1.70
visuals.caption: Observed first pi to pi star transition energies of polyenes plotted against calculated HOMO LUMO gaps. The correlation is a strong vote of confidence for the model.

visuals.label: Table 1.3
visuals.caption: HOMO energies of simple molecules from photoelectron spectroscopy. The ordering matters more than memorizing every entry.

visuals.label: Figure 1.71
visuals.caption: Energy levels of two nuclei showing no apparent coupling. It is the starting point for understanding spin splitting.

visuals.label: Figure 1.72
visuals.caption: Energy levels for positively coupled directly bonded nuclei. It explains why one bond couplings split signals into matched doublets.

visuals.label: Figure 1.73
visuals.caption: Energy levels for negatively coupled nuclei separated by two bonds. It shows how the same apparent splitting can arise with opposite sign.

equations.spoken: Equation 1 point 17 gives the HOMO LUMO separation for a representative linear polyene series.
equations.note: It links increasing conjugation length to a shrinking frontier orbital gap.

equations.spoken: Equation 1 point 18 is the McConnell relation for proton coupling in electron spin resonance.
equations.note: It links the observed splitting to the unpaired spin population on the adjacent carbon atom.
